messages.content: shorten：Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease characterized by the excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix components, presenting a significant challenge in the field of medicine. Despite extensive research efforts, effective therapeutic strategies targeting liver fibrosis remain limited. Thus, it is essential to explore innovative approaches for the management of this condition.

Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease with excessive build-up of matrix components, posing a challenge in medicine. Effective treatments are lacking, so discovering new approaches is crucial.

Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease with excessive accumulation of extracellular matrix components, posing a significant challenge in medicine. Despite extensive research efforts, effective treatments remain limited, highlighting the need for innovative approaches to manage this condition.
Liver fibrosis is a chronic and progressive disease with excessive matrix build-up, challenging medicine. Effective treatments are limited, necessitating innovative management approaches.

shorten： C57BL/6J male mice were used to model liver fibrosis, induced by Carbon tetrachloride (CCl4). The fibrotic mice were treated with interleukin-10 (IL-10) gene and cleared NK cells with an Asialo GM1 (ASGM1) antibody. The mice were divided into five groups: normal controls (NORMAL group), liver fibrosis group (FIBROSIS group), IL-10 gene treatment group (IL-10 group), NK cells clearance group (FIBROSIS/ASGM1 group) and IL-10 gene treatment combined with NK cell clearance group (IL-10/ASGM1 group).

C57BL/6J male mice were used as a liver fibrosis model, induced by CCl4. Fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and ASGM1 antibody to clear NK cells. The mice were divided into five groups: NORMAL, FIBROSIS, IL-10, FIBROSIS/ASGM1, and IL-10/ASGM1.
shorten：To observe the effect of the presence or absence of NK cells on the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10，fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and cleared NK cells using ASGM1 antibody.
To study the impact of NK cells on the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10, fibrotic mice were treated with IL-10 gene and NK cells were cleared using ASGM1 antibody.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, promoting the NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene effectively reduces liver damage and ameliorates liver fibrosis in mice. Additionally, it enhances the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, leading to increased expression of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a, and promoting NK cell development. IL-10 also regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and elevates the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, depletion of NK cells from the liver markedly impedes the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and promotes NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

The presence of NK cells enhances the beneficial effects of the IL-10 gene in mice, leading to reduced liver damage and improved liver fibrosis. IL-10 gene expression promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver, stimulating the upregulation of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a expression, thereby promoting NK cell development. It also regulates the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, depletion of NK cells from the liver significantly impairs the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10.

improve：In the presence of NK cells, the IL-10 gene significantly alleviates liver damage and improves liver fibrosis in mice.
At the same time, the IL-10 gene promotes the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and promotes NKG2D and IFN of NK cells- γ Upregulation of CD107a expression and promotion of NK cell development. In addition, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5 in the liver. However, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 is significantly inhibited.

The presence of NK cells enhances the effectiveness of the IL-10 gene in ameliorating liver damage and improving liver fibrosis in mice. The IL-10 gene stimulates the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and facilitates the upregulation of NKG2D, IFN-γ, and CD107a expression, consequently promoting NK cell development. Additionally, it regulates the migration of classical NK cells to the fibrotic liver and increases the level of CCL5. Conversely, when NK cells are depleted from the liver, the beneficial effects of IL-10 on fibrosis reduction are notably hindered.

improve:In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention significantly alleviated liver injury and collagen fiber deposition, improving mouse liver fibrosis. In addition, it promoted the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and enhanced their function, as evidenced by an increase in activation-related receptor NKG2D and toxic factors IFN-γ and CD107a. At the same time, it also regulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver accompanied by an increase in CCL5 content in the liver. However, when NK cells were exhausted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited.

In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention effectively reduced liver injury and collagen deposition, improving liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention enhanced the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and boosted their function, as evidenced by increased expression of activation-related receptor NKG2D, as well as the production of inflammatory factors IFN-γ and cytotoxic factor CD107a. Furthermore, IL-10 gene intervention regulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues, accompanied by an elevation in the liver's CCL5 levels. However, when NK cells were depleted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly hindered.

Write a conclusion for these results:In the presence of NK cells, IL-10 gene intervention effectively reduced liver injury and collagen deposition, improving liver fibrosis in mice. This intervention enhanced the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and boosted their function, as evidenced by increased expression of activation-related receptor NKG2D, as well as the production of inflammatory factors IFN-γ and cytotoxic factor CD107a. Furthermore, IL-10 gene intervention regulated the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues, accompanied by an elevation in CCL5 levels. However, when NK cells were depleted from the liver, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited.

In conclusion, our findings demonstrate that IL-10 gene intervention, in the presence of NK cells, effectively improves liver fibrosis by reducing liver injury and collagen deposition in mice. This intervention enhances the accumulation of NK cells in the liver and enhances their functional capacity, as indicated by increased expression of activation-related receptor NKG2D and the secretion of key inflammatory and cytotoxic factors IFN-γ and CD107a. The IL-10 gene also regulates the migration of classical NK cells to fibrotic liver tissues by increasing the expression of liver chemokine CCL5. However, the depletion of NK cells from the liver significantly hampers the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10. These findings highlight the potential therapeutic value of targeting NK cells and utilizing IL-10 gene therapy in the management of liver fibrosis.

improve：The anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 is dependent on enhancing the function of natural killer cells in mouse fibrotic liver, via promoting NK cells activation, cytotoxicity, development and migration, highlighting the potential of targeting NK cells in IL-10 gene intervention as a therapeutic approach for the management of liver fibrosis.

In conclusion, our results highlight the crucial role of natural killer (NK) cells in mediating the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 in mouse liver fibrosis. IL-10 gene intervention enhances the activation, cytotoxicity, development, and migration of NK cells within the fibrotic liver microenvironment. These findings underscore the therapeutic potential of targeting NK cells in IL-10 gene intervention as a promising approach for the management of liver fibrosis.

improve：The anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 is dependent on enhancing the function of natural killer cells in mouse fibrotic liver, by promoting NK cells activation, cytotoxicity, development and migration, elucidating the mechanisms of IL-10 regulating NK cells to mitigate liver fibrosis progression.

In conclusion, our findings elucidate the crucial role of natural killer (NK) cells in mediating the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 in a mouse model of liver fibrosis. IL-10 treatment enhances the activation, cytotoxicity, development, and migration of NK cells within the fibrotic liver. These results provide valuable insights into the mechanisms by which IL-10 regulates NK cells to mitigate the progression of liver fibrosis.

shorten:Liver damage was assessed by hematoxylin-eosin (H&E) staining and plasma alanine aminotransferase (ALT) and aspartate aminotransferase (AST) levels. Liver fibrosis was visualized by Sirius Red staining and hydroxyproline assay. The distribution of NK cells in liver tissue was analyzed through immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence. The evaluation of NK cells isolated from liver tissue or blood, such as quantity, activation, cytotoxicity, maturity degrees, and sources, was performed using flow cytometry. The chemotactic factors in fibrotic liver were determined by enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA).

Liver damage was assessed through H&E staining and plasma ALT/AST levels. Liver fibrosis was evaluated using Sirius Red staining and hydroxyproline assay. NK cell distribution in liver tissue was analyzed through immunohistochemistry and immunofluorescence. Flow cytometry was utilized to evaluate NK cell quantity, activation, cytotoxicity, maturity degrees, and sources in liver tissue/blood. Chemotactic factors in fibrotic liver were determined via ELISA.
improve：However, when NK cells were depleted from the liver, carbon tetrachloride induced liver fibrosis injury was more severe, and the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited

When NK cells were depleted from the liver, the severity of liver fibrosis injury induced by carbon tetrachloride increased. Furthermore, the depletion of NK cells significantly hindered the ability of IL-10 to exert its anti-fibrotic effect.
improve：However, when NK cells were depleted from the liver, carbon tetrachloride induced liver fibrosis injury was more severe, and the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10 was significantly inhibited

Depletion of NK cells from the liver resulted in exacerbated liver fibrosis injury induced by carbon tetrachloride, leading to a more severe condition. Moreover, the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 was substantially compromised in the absence of NK cells.
